Okay, so before we get you in the conversation, I'm gonna give Lauren a chance to throw a couple of ideas, but I wanna answer this question that you asked at the end, which is, how do you figure this stuff out? This is the hardest question. This is the question, what in the world do I do? I would love to tell you that I came up with the answer, or I didn't come up with this answer, but it is by far the most reliable thing that I do. I do this every day, I do it for every aspect of my life, I do it with every client that I have. I'm staring at a legal pad on my desk now that is just pages and pages and pages of evidence of me doing this. It is an idea that I got from Josh Waitzkin, and he's done several interviews with Tim Ferriss, Josh Waitzkin was a chess prodigy, he is the subject of the book in the movie Searching for Bobby Fisher, phenomenally intelligent person, very interesting person, incredible avid learner, and he does coaching, like super high end coaching with ultra high performers like hedge fund managers and other things, and he has this exercise he does, or it's sort of a discipline he does with his coaching clients, which he calls the most important question. And the idea behind this is in any situation, whatever it is, there is the most important question that needs to be answered. And oftentimes we think we know what the question that we're answering, but either it's not sharp enough, we haven't done the work to really narrow in on a very specific question, that's the most important thing that we need to do, or we're answering a question, but our gut deep down, we know really that's not actually the question we're supposed to be answering, and when we know instinctively, intuitively, that's not the right question, our motivation to answer the question drops. So the way I do, and my method is not, the method I don't think matters as much, my method is I sit down with a pencil, for some reason the pencil is lower barrier to me, it's just a pencil who cares, I'm just scratching on a page, I sit down with a legal pad or a stack of paper or a post-it or whatever I've got, and I write whatever the question is that comes to my mind. Sometimes it's just me orienting myself, like I will say, what do I need to know about X? So I might in this example, Valentina, if I were you in this example, I might sit down and I might write this main question, how do I know where to start? How do I, and then I just let whatever shows up appear on the page at first, this isn't like a free writing exercise, but what I begin to realize is that that question I have has a lot of questions associated with it, and I kind of have a dialogue with myself, and I might read something like, or I might write something like, how do I know which offer to start? Well, I have such and such for this offer, but I don't really love that offer, or I'm worried about that offer being too expensive, and then I might reorient to a new question, how do I know if that offer is too expensive? And I look at this, and that's not really the most important question, but I just keep doing that and doing that, and for whatever reason, or however this works from a creative process, at some point I start getting on the trail, and I start, and I'll find myself, I'll write a question, and I'm like, that's it, and then sometimes I think, well, that's almost it, and then I rewrite it, but it within generally no more than 10 minutes, and I do that in 10 minutes, be aware I've done this thousands of times, so at first it wasn't 10 minutes, 15, 20, 30, 40 minutes, whatever, but I don't let myself get up from that session until I have written what I believe is the question I have to answer next, and sometimes maybe it wasn't the most important question, but it is the question that is preventing me from taking action, the question that's preventing me from moving forward toward the thing that I'm trying to solve, I've now have written it down with clarity, and all of my effort then goes into solving that question, into focusing on that question, and when I get my answer, I set in motion a process, and things start to happen, and this, it is the most reliable thing that I do, it is so effective, so for you, I would, and I don't know, and we can talk about this when we get you in a second here, but I don't know where you would approach this, I can't do your stream of consciousness, but you have a lot of products, so I would be asking questions about which product do I feel like has the most opportunity, what about this scares me the most? That's a great question to ask yourself, because often what we find is there is a, or often what I find is that there is a resistance underneath, there's something that's not allowing us to take action, and that's something that's based on fear, and it doesn't have to be fear, like I'm terrified the battle of the world's going to end, it can be fear of embarrassment, it can be fear of, I don't know, it can be fear of wasting money, it can be fear of wasting time, you might, when you go down the fear road, you might realize like I'm just really, I'm concerned about making a mistake and putting a lot of time and energy into the wrong thing, how do I know, how do I address that fear? Well, the answer is take action, right? Wondering about what's going to happen, is it going to tell you what's going to happen? And for me, sometimes just seeing that that's holding me back becomes the thing that allows me to move forward, I'm like, oh, I get it, I'm scared, and I'm just kind of scared to take action, or I get it, I'm thinking about 10 things at once, and I can't concentrate on this one thing, so like once I've named it, it is now object instead of me being subject to it, it is something I can look at and say, oh, that's the self-doubt thing showing up, I'm not sure this thing that I'm writing is the best thing I've ever created, and I don't like how that feels, oh, all right, now I know what that is, now that I've named it, I am no longer subject to it, it is now object to me. So with all of that as background, let's have you jump on, Valentina, I think Andre, if you can unmute her, and we can just sort of run whatever direction you want to run with and ask whatever questions you want to ask, and Lauren and I and Andre will just start having fun. And the ones who decide to go for the membership, it's because they cannot afford the one-on-one. I got you. Okay, so the membership is basically a down-sell to your one-on-one coaching. It just naturally works pretty well like that, but it actually provides a lot of value. Yeah, yeah. I would be thinking, I would be thinking, any of those free ways that you got people into your list, is that something you could bundle? You could turn into a digital product that you could sell for a front-end. That might be something that would allow you to pay for your traffic. And then you would be getting leads essentially for free that you could drive into your membership in your one-on-one workshop. That might help ease the financial issues of paying for paid traffic. That's just one potential avenue. But I think what I would be looking at to think of the biggest question to ask, and to go back to kind of Sean's framework, I would be looking at what could you offer as a front-end, whether that is a paid front-end or whether that's a free front-end, what could you offer as a front-end that would as powerfully as possible set up a connection to your membership? What would provide real value as a front-end but yet be incomplete enough that someone would want to pay to complete that offering by upgrading to your membership? I would think that is the biggest question to answer because you get that offer down and everything else takes care of itself. Yeah, I do believe that the workshop does that. Even though it looks like, yeah, it's really hard to get people to show up in a live workshop. And I do believe that the challenge would do that even better because they would be able to work with me for a week and see how that feels and see that they actually make progress in their life. And then that's very hard then to step away from that when there is a connection. Does the workshop need to be live? No, it doesn't need to be live right now. I'm creating a funnel in which it is evergreen and it's a long copy, add straight to the page in which there is, they want our workshop and then there is just a very straight question, where is your journey out of anxiety leading you? And then they have, they can start the application take it further. So they have a questionnaire to fill out and that I would get back to them when they fill out the questionnaires just to pre-qualify people. I guess they opt in to see the presentation? No, they don't opt in is free. It's all free, they don't need to opt in. But if when they fill out the questionnaire, then they are opting in. Okay, one of the things I, and I think you're on track to do this, but we had a client that did this years ago and it made such a huge difference. But they created really great content and people who saw the content just raved about it. This video was like presentation format. The problem was they put the content behind an opt-in wall and it was just a nightmare to get people to opt in. We just switched the order. You see the content first, opt in for more after and that changed everything. And I think for you, if you know you have this device, this workshop that is a precursor to creating customers, then make that available for free and encourage the consumption of it. Like we want people to watch that. That is effectively your multi-page presale site. It's doing that work for you after that. Then it's a matter of, there's sort of a couple of different ways you can approach it after that. You could then go from workshop to, because what we're trying to do is we're trying to increase the price of engagement gradually instead of increasing the price of engagement in a way that feels uncomfortable. So very easy to click on an ad. You read an ad, they're just giving attention. That's very low engagement. We're now sending somebody to a one-hour presentation. That's significantly more engagement. But we haven't put any barriers in the way. This is just the next logical step. So after that, now we can increase the requirements for engagement a little bit. I wouldn't necessarily go to an application. At that point, I would do something more that is continue the journey. So you might say, you've got a seven day challenge or whatever the next thing is that builds on what they've already consumed. So they've already engaged with the ad, they've already engaged with your workshop. What's the next thing that's going to move them closer to you? Whatever that is, and I wouldn't do an application at this point, like you haven't built up enough trust and authority with them yet to move to starting asking those questions. And I get that you can, and a lot of people say that that's appropriate. I'm not suggesting that that's not true. I'm just saying that I think for you, you're doing the show and tell version. And I love that where it's like, let me just show you what I do, how it can help you, give them ways to participate in that. And then at some point, it's going, the transition is going to happen where the next thing there is a financial investment. And you could do, there are lots of business models for this. I'm thinking of Jonathan Andre, and his model is interesting here, where you could do a low-cost month one trial. And after that, there's sort of a couple of ways you can do it after that. If you know that getting people into month one creates lots of customers, lower the barrier to getting people into month one. So have them watch the webinar, show them the opportunities to maybe do a $10 first month free trial or a $10 seven day trial, whatever it is for the membership. And you can get more sophisticated over time. You can do, when they sign up for that, you can do, you can make an offer right then that they can upgrade to some version that's paid at a discounted rate. So when it renew, if they say you give them the first month for $10, they could immediately, and it's going to be $100 a month after that, you can do month one is $10 and immediately after, they can lock in the price for $75 a month if they start, if they want to just purchase that right now. And you don't need to do that. So that's just all business model stuff that you can do. But it's this, think about stairs, right? There are lots of different ways you can build a staircase. And you can make a staircase really steep and you can also make a staircase with really high steps, or you can make a nice gradual, almost a ramp where it doesn't even feel like you're climbing the stairs, that it's, you engage with an ad, you watched, and you might even break out that one hour workshop into three parts, for example, whatever it takes, but get people to move closer and closer to you where you are pulling them closer and closer to you so that when it is appropriate, when you've earned their trust and they see the value because you've led with value, that then you make the offer for the thing that costs money, that's a very powerful way to approach this. Any thoughts to add here? One, something that Seth said a long time ago that often turn it last to the point where it's now a signature on one of our support emails. Is motivating the committed, our performance persuading the uncommitted. And it's something that I'm always thinking of whenever I'm deciding to build anything. And I only want committed people and I want to filter out those people that are uncommitted. And the reality is most people are uncommitted. They can talk the talk, but as soon as action is required, they're uncommitted. And there's a lot of those people. So everything I do from the free stuff, especially the free stuff is about pulling committed people forward and for training that uncommitted. And it's, it's fairly easy to filter out uncommitted. They just fall away pretty quickly. And then what you left with all those people that are uncommitted, I mean, that are committed. And you really want to figure out how in the world did they find you? And self-reporting is not going to be 100% accurate. But you're going to hear nuances. Like, but it was the, they were exposed because of the competitor. And so now you know, like their frame of mind, like, oh, these are people who are, they saw like the whoever's doing this. And I'm making this up, but that's what the interviews will reveal. And you, what you don't want to do when you interview is ask like 10 scripted questions. You want to ask one to two questions that are easy to answer to kind of get the conversation going and then you want to ask open-ended questions and really let them tell you. You've got to build a little rapport and trust first. So ask, you know, kind of build a little momentum, momentum, ask maybe two, maybe three straightforward questions, easy to answer that you care about. Then, and one of those questions might be, you know, how has your experience been since whatever? You know, and then because when they, you know, how, how, how, how, what has your experience been since you, you know, purchased the course? Did you set it up? You know, all these other things, because what you might find is they might tell you benefits that you had no idea were benefits. Like, which allows you to speak to, to that audience. But this is, this shouldn't, this is like a week of work, maybe 10 days. Don't make this a, you know, and I'm saying this to myself, not to you. Don't make this an excuse to procrastinate, but get the emails out, send them personally from you each time. Ask for no more than 10 minutes of their time. Many people will talk to you for 30 minutes, but make the ask small. Don't say five minutes, they won't believe it. Say 10. You know, can I get 10 minutes of your time to talk about whatever, if you prefer not to speak on the phone. And I'd be very clear, I'd love to ask you a few questions on the phone if you would prefer Skype or Zoom, whatever. If you prefer not to do that, and then ask three open-ended questions, and thank them or whatever else. You will find so much gold in these conversations. You will not believe the insights you get from these conversations. You're going to find, you're going to hear the language they use, the process they use, the thing that inspired them. Much of this will be useful later when you do Facebook, but the audience that you're speaking to searched, so have them tell you how to construct your search campaigns. Does that make sense to you to see where, where you can run with that? Yeah, no, that makes absolutely a ton of sense. I mean, the only question I really had was like, how do I figure out what they're searching for? But that, that whole thing answered it. Do you have any... Go ahead. Sorry, I was just going to interject quickly. You can, you can also use tools like AHRF. I mean, if your website is ranking for organic search terms, although you can't extract that, that search data from your analytics because it's being stripped out, you can use third party tools like AHRFs that will do a pretty good job of figuring out what keyword phrases you're ranking for. And then it'll tell you where you're ranking for it. And then with a bit of, you know, deductive reasoning, you can figure out which ones are, you know, which, which pool of keywords are the ones that are probably driving most of your organic traffic. And then you just spin up your Google Ads campaign and stick those keywords in and give it a go, see if you can scale it up. So that's another quick way, I think, easy one, perhaps. Cool. I'll do that. And then I thought I remember you guys talking about early on the traffic engine, like you don't want to send like Google search traffic straight to kind of like a webinar opt-in page. Is that true? Yeah, I mean, I don't, the challenge, so this is the challenge with opt-ins and search. Google wants a, the thing that Google is looking at most to determine your quality score is, I mean, click through rate is huge, the quality of your ad. But then what happens when somebody gets there? So if you think about, if you're Google and you don't see a person and you don't, you don't see, like all you see is an ad is shown, there's a click on an ad, there's a landing page, and then you know how long somebody stays. And that then tells you, like that's all you can, obviously Google has more information, but like those are the big pieces of data in that you notice that somebody's bidding on this term. And then the behavior that follows after is the person hits the page and then a few seconds is gone, which is very common with an opt-in. 80% or more, they're gonna bail. I mean, 20% opt-in rate on a webinar opt-in page is pretty decent. So that signal that goes back to Google is basically says this is a low quality match because somebody, you know, this term, the advertiser said, I'm relevant for this term, but the user behavior says no, they're not. So, and that's how Google's regular search works too. If lots of people search for, you know, how to grow a garden indoors and they click on a number one link and they bail, that link stops being number one very quickly because Google's like, okay, that's obviously not the right match. We kind of got something wrong. So when you put an opt-in in, you're introducing a barrier to Google getting a good signal that will drive your advertising cost down and get you more traffic. So there are two ways to handle this. Version number one is you put the opt-in low on the page and you sell the opt-in on the landing page with really valuable, this is what you're going to expect. This is what we're going to cover. You put an Easter egg or two in that, like, did you know kind of stuff? Don't make overblown financial claims or other things, but you put a thousand words of content, 800 words of content, whatever, on the landing page that explains what it is that you're going to be offering them. Kind of the overview of what they're going to get. One kind of neat trick I've seen other people use is you, before the opt-in, you make available the workbook that they use and you let them see it. They can just download the PDF. Like here's the workbook for the webinar or whatever you call it, master class, whatever, overview. And the workbook is actually a sales piece and it's not an overt sales piece, but it'll stay things like page one will be, the question might say something like, the top three most important, the top three most common questions we get are about, an IV nutritional clinic are one, two, and three. Now that's a bad example, let me give you a better example. It's more like insights. So you're telling them what you're going to tell them. The top three most important things to know about XR and then you put one, two, three, and then there's a blank line next to each where the implication is they're going to fill that out themselves. So what the PDF becomes is a guidebook that says implicitly, you're going to, these are all the things we're going to cover in this two hour class or whatever it is. And those are all the things that they really care about. You might, and you might break it into sections. You might have, however your webinar is broken out, you might have the benefits of the, the benefits and expectations, a financial calculator, legal considerations, the checklist of things that you need to do. All of that is in the PDF. The PDF is available for free before the opt-in. You can note parenthetically that they'll also get it after they opt-in, but then what's, what will happen, and I've seen this happen a bunch, and it's really interesting. People hit the landing page, they get, they read the content and it's good. They get to the part where there's the PDF link. They open the PDF, you have it open in a new tab, opens a new tab, they haven't left the other page though. So that they are then, they look at it quickly, they're like, oh, this looks really interesting. They go back to the other page, then they go down and they opt-in. So they've stayed longer on that page. It's not gaming the system, because you're giving them good content, but you're keeping them on that page. That's like, that's a long answer to a short question, but that's a really good way to avoid the penalty of search ad to opt-in. That you need to put content in there, wrap the content to give context, to give out, to give value, increase the value and the desire by showing them what they're going to get in the webinar, which also is a service to them, because if it's not interesting, they can bail, and then give the webinar, and you will amplify your sign-up rates for the webinar. That's how I would orchestrate that. That's really interesting. So you just say to give, like, just put a link on there, here's the e-book. Yeah, we have an option to, like, watch the, it's using, like, the webinar jam software. Okay, I got you. But now. Yeah, I mean, I wouldn't mix those two things as being the same thing. So I wouldn't offer the e-book on that landing page, because then it becomes a distraction. What I would do, the reason I was asking about it, if it's just in time, is if there's any lag between the webinar and the, and the, so between when they sign up in the webinar, I would make it available then to, okay, your webinar's in two days, and in the meantime, here's a free e-book that you're going to find interesting. Like, that's one way to do it. But what you're really trying to do with the PDF on the, you're trying to do two things with the PDF workbook. The first is you're trying to amplify desire. And it's, and you're doing this very ethically. Like, I don't want to have anyone think that this is like a, some persuasion hack. It's not, you're making an outline of the, it could be five pages, six pages, 15 pages. You're making an outline of what you're going to go over that you believe is what they care about most. And you're forcing, you're giving them an opportunity where they have a resource where they can take notes, but you've structured it in a way so that they don't have to take 50 pages of notes. They can just fill in the things that you're already saying are most important. And that has the dual effect of building desire and being a service to the customer where they can look. Because if I look at that, I'm like, this isn't really what I'm looking for. Well, then you've told me, I don't need to sit through the webinar to find that out. But if I look at that, if that, I look at that workbook and I think, this is exactly what I'm looking for. Then that has done a service to me effectively. That has told me to stay, given me a reason to stay. I think that's better than the, in this example, that's better than the ebook thing. And if someone opts in, they don't watch the webinar or show them the ebook, get them interested again, get a second bite at the apple. But I wouldn't mix those two things. Like when you look at it under, like from a linear reductionist point of view, you're like, yeah, what really, what difference is that gonna make? This is something I've seen make seismic changes in funnels where that one addition just unlocked so much because it signals in a way to your audience a depth of interest in their wellbeing, a depth of concern in their wellbeing, and that you're also not trying to get everybody under the sun to go to the next step. You're really making sure it's right for them. And that has an emergent effect far beyond the amount of time it takes to create. Like I can't stress that enough.
